definition: The arising or originating condition of something, especially the origin of dissatisfaction in the four noble truths.
notes: The project prefers origin in four-truth contexts because it reads cleanly in English and marks the explanatory role of the term without sounding overly process-heavy. Arising remains acceptable when the sentence emphasizes dynamic emergence rather than analytic designation. The term should be heard both as a truth-label and as part of the wider causal vocabulary of the teachings.

context_rules.context: In the formula of the four noble truths
In explanatory prose focusing on emergence or coming-to-be
context_rules.notes: Use when the dynamic aspect matters more than the doctrinal label.

context_rules.context: When the passage emphasizes explanatory origin within causal analysis rather than mere temporal appearance
context_rules.notes: Keep the term analytically strong without over-metaphysicizing it.

example_phrases.translation: this is the noble truth of the origin of dissatisfaction
craving is what leads to renewed becoming
example_phrases.notes: Useful doctrinal line showing why taṇhā is identified as the origin.
